termsContent.intro: By using ClawStudiouo, you agree to the following terms:
items: **Permitted Use.** This website and its tools are provided for lawful purposes only. You may use them to process files you own or have the rights to, or publicly accessible files.

**Prohibited Use.** You may not use this website to process content that is illegal, infringing, or violates third-party intellectual property rights. Automated abuse, circumvention of usage limits, or attempts to overload the service are prohibited.
**No Storage.** ClawStudiouo does not store uploaded files or processed output. All processing occurs in-memory and data is discarded after each request.
**No Warranty.** All tools are provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. Output quality depends on the input files and selected parameters.
**Limitation of Liability.** The provider is not liable for any direct, indirect, or consequential damages arising from use of this website or its tools.
**Changes.** These terms and the website functionality may be updated at any time. Continued use after changes constitutes acceptance.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.intro: Welcome to ClawStudiouo (hereinafter referred to as "this website"). We take your privacy very seriously. This policy explains how we collect, use, and protect your personal information.

section1.title: 1. Information We Collect
section1.item1: When you use our image conversion tools (such as GIF to Spritesheet), we do not permanently store your original files on our servers. All uploaded images are used for real-time processing only and are automatically deleted shortly after processing is complete.
section1.item2: We record your IP address, browser type, visit time, and pages clicked for website traffic analysis and user experience optimization.

section2.title: 2. Use of Cookies
section2.intro: This website uses cookies to store certain information and provide better services:
section2.item1: Used to record your usage preferences (such as tool settings and usage count).
section2.item2: We partner with third-party providers such as Google to serve ads based on your past visits to this website or other websites through cookies.

section3.title: 3. Google AdSense & Third-Party Advertising
section3.item1: Google, as a third-party provider, uses cookies to serve ads on this website.
section3.item2: Google's use of advertising cookies enables Google and its partners to serve ads to users based on their visits to this website and/or other websites on the internet.
section3.item3: You can opt out of personalized advertising by visiting the

section4.title: 4. Data Security
section4.content: We take standard security measures (such as SSL encrypted transmission) to protect your data. However, internet transmission is not 100% secure, and we cannot guarantee absolute security.

section5.title: 5. Third-Party Links
section5.content: This website may contain links to other websites (such as RapidAPI). Please note that we are not responsible for the privacy practices of these external websites, and we recommend that you read their respective privacy policies.

section6.title: 6. Your Rights
section6.content: Under applicable laws (such as GDPR), you have the right to request access to, correction of, or deletion of personal data we hold about you. If you have any questions, please contact us using the information below.

section7.title: 7. Contact Us
section7.content: If you have any questions about this privacy policy, please email:

waitingRoom.title: Your Download
tips: Use the **Padding** option to add pixel gaps between frames in your sprite sheet. This prevents texture bleeding — a common rendering artifact in game engines where adjacent frames leak into each other.
Enable **Remove Background** to automatically detect and remove solid-color backgrounds from each frame. The algorithm samples the four corner pixels to find the background color.

The hex color picker includes an **eyedropper tool** — click it to pick any color directly from the preview image or anywhere on your screen. This is perfect for selecting the exact background color when auto-detection doesn't match.
Adjust the **Tolerance** slider to control how aggressively similar colors are removed. A value of 30 works for most clean backgrounds, while 50–80 handles gradients or compression artifacts.
waitingRoom.apiPromo: Need to automate this? The Easy GIF2Sprite API on RapidAPI lets you integrate the same conversion engine into your own applications and workflows.

api.title: Use GIF to PNG Sprite Sheet API
api.content: Want to integrate GIF-to-spritesheet conversion into your own application? The Easy GIF2Sprite API provides the same powerful conversion engine behind this tool as a RESTful API. Upload a GIF file or pass a URL, and receive a PNG sprite sheet or individual frames in seconds — all via a simple HTTP request.
api.content2: The API supports all the features available in this tool: custom grid layouts, adjustable frame padding, automatic background removal, and frame extraction to ZIP. It's ideal for game asset pipelines, automated sprite generation workflows, batch processing tools, and any application that needs programmatic GIF-to-PNG conversion.

features.title: API Highlights
features.input: Accept file uploads or public GIF URLs
features.params: Full parameter control: columns, padding, background removal, tolerance
features.limit: Supports GIF files up to 20 MB
features.formats: Compatible with both GIF87a and GIF89a standards
features.response: Fast response times with Cloud Run auto-scaling

whatIs.title: What Is a Sprite Sheet?
whatIs.content: A sprite sheet (also called a spritesheet or texture atlas) is a single image file that contains multiple smaller images — typically animation frames — arranged in a grid. Instead of loading dozens of individual image files, game engines and web browsers load one sprite sheet and display each frame by shifting the visible region. This dramatically reduces HTTP requests, lowers memory overhead, and keeps your animations smooth and efficient.
whatIs.content2: Sprite sheets are a fundamental technique in 2D game development, CSS animation, and motion graphics. Engines like Unity, Godot, Phaser, and PixiJS all natively support sprite sheets. On the web, CSS developers use the background-position property to step through frames, creating lightweight animations without JavaScript or video files.

whatIsFrames.title: What Is Frame Extraction?
whatIsFrames.content: Frame extraction is the process of splitting a GIF animation into its individual frames, each saved as a separate PNG image. This is useful when you need to edit specific frames, re-sequence an animation, or import frames individually into a game engine or video editor.
whatIsFrames.content2: Our tool packages all extracted frames into a single ZIP archive for convenient download. Each frame is named with a zero-padded index (e.g., frame_00.png, frame_01.png) so they sort correctly in any file manager or import dialog.

howTo.title: How to Convert a GIF to a Sprite Sheet

step1.title: 1. Choose Your Output Mode
step1.content: Select "Spritesheet" to combine all frames into a single PNG grid, or "Frames" to extract every frame as an individual PNG packed in a ZIP file.

step2.title: 2. Upload Your GIF
step2.content: Drag and drop your GIF file onto the upload area, or click to open the file picker. You can also paste a publicly accessible GIF URL if your file is hosted online. We accept both GIF87a and GIF89a formats up to 20 MB.

step3.title: 3. Configure Options (Optional)
step3.content: Expand the Advanced Options panel to fine-tune your output. For sprite sheets, you can set the number of columns in the grid and the pixel padding between frames. You can also enable automatic background removal, which uses a smart algorithm to detect and remove solid-color backgrounds from each frame.

step4.title: 4. Download Your Result
step4.content: Once conversion is complete, sprite sheets are displayed as a full-width preview image that you can inspect before downloading. For frame extraction, the ZIP file downloads automatically. Click "Convert Another GIF" to start over with a new file.

spritesheet.title: Sprite Sheet Generation
spritesheet.content: Combine all GIF frames into a single PNG image arranged in a customizable grid. The default layout calculates the closest-to-square arrangement automatically, or you can specify the exact number of columns.

frames.title: Individual Frame Extraction
frames.content: Extract every frame from a GIF animation as a separate PNG file. All frames are packaged in a ZIP archive with zero-padded filenames for correct sorting.

columns.title: Custom Grid Layout
columns.content: Control how frames are arranged in your sprite sheet by specifying the number of columns. Leave it on auto to get a balanced, near-square grid, or set a specific number for horizontal strip layouts (1 row) or vertical strips (1 column).

padding.title: Frame Padding
padding.content: Add pixel gaps between frames in your sprite sheet. Padding prevents texture bleeding — a common artifact in game engines where adjacent frames leak pixels into each other during rendering. A padding of 1–2 px is usually sufficient.

removeBg.title: Automatic Background Removal
removeBg.content: Remove solid-color backgrounds from each frame before compositing. The algorithm samples the four corner pixels of each frame to auto-detect the background color, then uses a flood-fill from all corners to make matching pixels transparent. Adjust the tolerance slider (0–255) to control how aggressively similar colors are removed.

url.title: Convert from URL
url.content: Don't have the GIF file on your device? Paste a publicly accessible URL and we'll fetch and convert it directly. This is especially handy on mobile devices or when working with GIFs hosted on image sharing platforms.

privacy.title: Privacy First
privacy.content: Your files are processed on the server and never stored. Once the converted result is returned to your browser, the original upload is immediately discarded. No registration, no tracking, no data retention.

gamedev.title: 2D Game Development
gamedev.content: Sprite sheets are the standard format for 2D character animations, particle effects, and UI elements in game engines. Convert your GIF mockups or pixel art animations into engine-ready sprite sheets for Unity, Godot, Phaser, GameMaker, or RPG Maker. Set custom column counts and padding to match your engine's import requirements.

css.title: CSS Sprite Animations
css.content: Create lightweight CSS animations without video files or JavaScript libraries. Use a sprite sheet as a CSS background image and animate it with the steps() timing function and background-position. This technique is widely used for loading spinners, animated icons, and micro-interactions on the web.

motionGraphics.title: Motion Graphics & Video Editing
motionGraphics.content: Extract individual frames from a GIF to import into video editors like After Effects, Premiere Pro, or DaVinci Resolve. Frame extraction gives you full control to re-time, recolor, or composite each frame independently.

emotes.title: Emotes & Sticker Sheets
emotes.content: Combine animated emotes or sticker frames into a single sprite sheet for chat platforms, streaming overlays, or social media tools. The auto-background-removal feature cleanly separates characters from solid-color backgrounds.

formats.a: We accept GIF files only (both GIF87a and GIF89a standards). The output is always PNG — either a single PNG sprite sheet or multiple PNGs in a ZIP archive.

maxSize.q: What is the maximum file size?
maxSize.a: The maximum input file size is 20 MB. This applies to both direct file uploads and GIF URLs. Most animated GIFs fall well within this limit.

maxFrames.q: Is there a limit on the number of frames?
maxFrames.a: There is no hard limit on frame count. However, GIFs with hundreds of frames will produce very large sprite sheets. For extremely long animations, consider using the Frames mode to extract individual PNGs instead.

transparency.a: Yes. If your original GIF has transparent regions, they are preserved in the output PNG. If you enable background removal, the detected background color is replaced with transparency.

autoColumns.q: How does the auto column layout work?
autoColumns.a: When you leave the Columns field empty, the tool calculates the number of columns as the ceiling of the square root of the total frame count. This produces the closest-to-square grid layout, which is optimal for texture memory and easy to work with in most game engines.

bgRemoval.q: How does automatic background removal work?
bgRemoval.a: The algorithm samples the RGBA color values of the four corner pixels of each frame. If all four corners are within the tolerance threshold of each other, their average color is used as the background target. A flood-fill starting from all four corners then removes all connected pixels within the tolerance range. If the corners are too different from each other, the frame is left unchanged to avoid removing foreground content.

tolerance.q: What tolerance value should I use?
tolerance.a: The default value of 30 works well for most GIFs with clean, solid-color backgrounds. For GIFs with slight gradients or compression artifacts in the background, try increasing to 50–80. Values above 100 become aggressive and may remove foreground pixels that are similar to the background color.

free.a: Yes, this tool is completely free to use with no registration required.

api.a: Yes. This tool is powered by the Easy GIF2Sprite API, which is available on RapidAPI for developers who want to integrate GIF-to-sprite conversion into their own applications and workflows.

api.title: Use the PNG to GIF API
api.content: Integrate PNG-to-GIF conversion into your applications with the Easy GIF2Sprite API. Whether you need to assemble individual frames or slice a spritesheet, the API handles both workflows through two dedicated endpoints.
api.content2: Upload PNG frames or a spritesheet via a simple HTTP request and receive an animated GIF in seconds. Perfect for game asset pipelines, automated sprite workflows, and batch processing tools.

features.title: API Highlights
features.input: Upload PNG files or provide a public URL
features.params: Control duration, loop, frame ordering, grid slicing, and more
features.limit: 100 files per request or 20MB single file
features.response: Fast response with Cloud Run auto-scaling

whatIsFramesToGif.title: What Is PNG Frames to GIF Conversion?
whatIsFramesToGif.content: PNG frames to GIF conversion takes a series of individual PNG images and assembles them into a single animated GIF file. Each PNG becomes one frame of the animation, played in sequence at a specified speed. The process preserves transparency from the original PNG files, so characters or objects with transparent backgrounds animate cleanly without unwanted white borders or background artifacts.
whatIsFramesToGif.content2: This is the reverse of frame extraction — instead of splitting an animation apart, you're building one from scratch. It's the standard workflow for game developers, animators, and designers who create each frame individually and need to preview or export the final animation as a GIF. The tool supports up to 100 individual PNG files per conversion, with configurable frame duration ranging from 10 milliseconds to 10 seconds per frame, giving you precise control over animation timing.
whatIsFramesToGif.content3: When assembling frames, you can choose how to handle PNG files with mismatched dimensions. The tool offers three approaches: centering smaller frames on a transparent canvas sized to the largest frame, filling the background with a solid color, or flagging the mismatch as an error so you can fix it before converting. You can also enable filename-based ordering, which sorts frames by the numeric suffix in filenames (e.g., walk_0.png, walk_1.png, walk_2.png), ensuring your animation plays in the correct sequence regardless of upload order.

whatIsSpritesheetToGif.title: What Is Spritesheet to GIF Conversion?
whatIsSpritesheetToGif.content: Spritesheet to GIF conversion slices a single image containing multiple frames arranged in a grid (a spritesheet) and reassembles them into an animated GIF. You specify the grid layout — either by column/row count or pixel cell dimensions — and the tool extracts each cell as a frame. The tool reads frames left-to-right, top-to-bottom, following the standard spritesheet convention used by most game engines and animation tools.
whatIsSpritesheetToGif.content2: This is particularly useful for previewing spritesheet animations without a game engine, sharing sprite animations on platforms that only support GIF, or converting existing game assets into a format suitable for documentation, social media, or design reviews. You can also select specific row and column ranges to extract only a portion of the spritesheet, which is ideal when a single sheet contains multiple animation sequences — such as walk, run, and idle cycles arranged in different rows.
whatIsSpritesheetToGif.content3: The spritesheet slicing mode supports padding between cells, which accounts for the spacing some tools add between frames during export. You can also enable skip-empty mode to automatically detect and discard fully transparent cells, avoiding blank frames at the end of your animation. Whether your spritesheet was created in Aseprite, Photoshop, TexturePacker, or any other tool, the conversion process works with any standard grid-based layout.

whyConvert.title: Why Convert PNG to GIF?
whyConvert.content: GIF remains one of the most universally supported image formats on the internet. Unlike video files or animated PNGs (APNG), GIF animations play automatically in virtually every browser, email client, messaging app, and social media platform without requiring any plugins or JavaScript. This makes GIF the go-to format when you need a lightweight animation that simply works everywhere.
whyConvert.content2: Converting PNG frames or spritesheets to GIF is especially valuable when you want to share animations outside of their original context. Game developers can preview character animations without launching a game engine. Designers can embed animated UI mockups directly in documentation or Slack messages. Artists can showcase their pixel art on platforms that don't support spritesheet playback. In each case, GIF serves as a universal preview format that anyone can view instantly.
whyConvert.content3: GIF animations are also self-contained — a single file holds every frame, the timing information, and the loop count. There's no need for external players, JavaScript libraries, or CSS animations to make them work. This simplicity makes GIF ideal for README files, wikis, email newsletters, and any context where you need an animation that just plays on its own with zero dependencies.

formatsExplained.title: Understanding PNG vs GIF
formatsExplained.content: PNG and GIF are both raster image formats, but they serve different purposes. PNG supports millions of colors (24-bit or 32-bit with alpha transparency), lossless compression, and a single static frame. It's the preferred format for high-quality images, screenshots, and individual animation frames where color accuracy matters. GIF, on the other hand, is limited to a 256-color palette per frame but supports multiple frames in a single file, making it the classic format for simple animations.
formatsExplained.content2: The 256-color limitation of GIF means that converting from PNG may introduce slight color changes if your source images use a wide color range. For pixel art and illustrations with flat colors, this is rarely noticeable. For photographic or gradient-heavy content, the palette reduction can produce visible banding. The tool handles this conversion automatically, selecting the best 256-color palette for your specific frames.
formatsExplained.content3: Another key difference is transparency handling. PNG supports full alpha transparency with 256 levels of opacity, allowing smooth semi-transparent edges. GIF transparency is binary — each pixel is either fully transparent or fully opaque. This means soft edges or semi-transparent overlays in your PNG frames will be converted to hard edges in the GIF output. For best results with transparent GIF animations, use PNG source frames that already have clean, hard-edged transparency.

howTo.title: How to Convert PNG to GIF

step1.title: 1. Choose Your Input Mode
step1.content: Select "From Frames" if you have individual PNG files for each animation frame — for example, a set of exported frames from Aseprite, Photoshop, or any drawing tool. Select "From Spritesheet" if you have a single PNG image with all frames arranged in a grid, which is common for game assets and sprite animations. You can switch between modes at any time using the tabs at the top of the tool.

step2.title: 2. Upload Your Files
step2.content: For frames mode, drag and drop multiple PNG files onto the upload area or click to open a file picker. You can upload up to 100 files, with each file up to 5MB and a total combined size of 60MB. The uploaded files are listed with their filenames so you can verify the selection and remove individual files if needed. For spritesheet mode, upload a single PNG file or paste a public URL pointing to the spritesheet image. A preview of the spritesheet will appear so you can confirm it loaded correctly.

step3.title: 3. Configure Animation Settings
step3.content: Set the frame duration in milliseconds — this controls how long each frame is displayed. The default of 100ms gives a smooth 10 FPS animation, but you can go as fast as 10ms or as slow as 10,000ms per frame. Set the loop count to 0 for infinite looping, or a specific number to play the animation a fixed number of times. For frames mode, enable filename-based sorting if your files follow a naming convention like walk_0.png, walk_1.png, and choose how to handle frames with different dimensions. For spritesheet mode, specify columns and rows (or cell pixel dimensions), and optionally set column/row ranges to extract only a specific animation from a multi-animation sheet.

step4.title: 4. Convert and Download
step4.content: Click Convert to send your files to the server and generate the animated GIF. The conversion typically takes just a few seconds. Once complete, a live preview displays the animation so you can verify the timing, frame order, and overall look before downloading. If the result isn't right, adjust your settings and convert again. When you're satisfied, click Download to save the GIF to your device. Click "Convert Another" to clear everything and start a fresh conversion with new files.

frames.title: Multi-Frame Assembly
frames.content: Upload up to 100 individual PNG files and combine them into a single animated GIF. Frames are assembled in upload order, or optionally sorted by the numeric suffix in filenames. This makes it easy to batch-export frames from any drawing or animation tool and assemble them into a polished GIF without manual reordering.

spritesheet.title: Spritesheet Slicing
spritesheet.content: Automatically slice a spritesheet PNG into individual frames using grid mode (columns × rows) or cell mode (pixel dimensions). The tool supports padding between cells to account for spacing added by export tools like TexturePacker or Aseprite. You can also limit the conversion to specific rows and columns, making it possible to extract a single animation from a sheet that contains many.

duration.title: Adjustable Frame Duration
duration.content: Control animation speed by setting the frame duration from 10ms (very fast) to 10,000ms (10 seconds per frame). The default of 100ms produces a smooth 10 FPS animation suitable for most sprite previews. For cinematic sequences you might increase it to 200ms or more, while fast action animations often look best at 50-80ms per frame.

fileOrder.title: Filename-Based Ordering
fileOrder.content: Enable filename sorting to automatically order frames by the _N numeric suffix in filenames (e.g., walk_0.png, walk_1.png, walk_2.png), regardless of upload order. This is especially helpful when selecting files from a folder where the operating system may not preserve the original sequence, ensuring your animation always plays in the intended order.

resize.title: Dimension Mismatch Handling
resize.content: When frames have different sizes, choose how to handle them: center smaller frames on a transparent canvas sized to the largest frame, fill the extra space with a configurable background color, or return an error so you can standardize dimensions first. This flexibility means you don't need to manually resize every frame before converting — the tool adapts to your workflow.

rangeSelect.title: Range Selection
rangeSelect.content: Extract only a portion of a spritesheet by specifying column and row ranges (e.g., columns 0-3, rows 2-4). This is essential for spritesheets that pack multiple animation sequences into a single image. Instead of slicing the entire sheet, you can target exactly the walk cycle, attack animation, or idle loop you need and convert just that section into a GIF.

privacy.title: Privacy First
privacy.content: Your files are processed on the server and never stored permanently. Once the GIF is generated and returned to your browser, the original uploads are immediately discarded from server memory. No account registration is required, no cookies are used for tracking, and your image data is never shared with third parties or used for any purpose beyond the conversion you requested.

gamePreview.title: Game Animation Preview
gamePreview.content: Preview spritesheet animations as GIFs without loading them into a game engine. Share character walk cycles, attack sequences, particle effects, or UI transitions with your team in a universally viewable format. For example, an artist can export a spritesheet from Aseprite, convert it to GIF, and drop it into a Slack channel for feedback — no one needs to install any special software to see the animation in action.

socialMedia.title: Social Media & Portfolio
socialMedia.content: Convert your pixel art frames or spritesheet animations into GIFs for sharing on Twitter, Discord, Reddit, or your portfolio website. GIF is universally supported and auto-plays on most platforms, making it the best format for showcasing animated artwork where you want the viewer to see the motion immediately. Many game devs use this workflow to share daily progress — export today's frames, convert to GIF, and post a quick update showing a new character animation or environment effect.

documentation.title: Technical Documentation
documentation.content: Create animated diagrams, UI flow demos, or step-by-step visual guides from individual screenshots. Embed the resulting GIF directly in GitHub README files, Confluence pages, Notion docs, or any documentation site. This is particularly effective for showing multi-step workflows, before-and-after comparisons, or interactive UI components where a static screenshot cannot convey the full behavior.

prototyping.title: Design Prototyping
prototyping.content: Quickly assemble UI mockup frames into animated walkthroughs to test animation timing and transitions before committing to a full implementation in code. Export each screen state as a PNG, arrange them in sequence, and convert to GIF to create a rapid prototype that stakeholders can review. This approach is faster than building interactive prototypes for early-stage feedback, and the resulting GIF can be shared via email or embedded in presentation slides.

formats.a: The input must be PNG files — this applies to both modes. In frames mode, every uploaded file must be a PNG image. In spritesheet mode, the input must be a single PNG file (uploaded directly or via a public URL). The output is always an animated GIF. Other image formats like JPEG, WebP, or BMP are not supported as input, so you'll need to convert them to PNG first if necessary.

maxFiles.q: How many frames can I upload?
maxFiles.a: In frames mode, you can upload up to 100 individual PNG files. Each file can be up to 5MB, and the total combined size of all files must not exceed 60MB. In spritesheet mode, the single PNG file can be up to 20MB. These limits ensure fast processing times while accommodating most real-world animation and spritesheet assets.

frameOrder.a: By default, frames are ordered by the sequence in which they are uploaded. If you want automatic ordering, enable "Sort by Filename" to arrange them by the _N numeric suffix in filenames (e.g., frame_0.png, frame_1.png, frame_2.png). This is helpful when your operating system doesn't preserve file order during selection. For spritesheet mode, frames are always read left-to-right, top-to-bottom, following the standard spritesheet convention.

differentSizes.q: What happens if my frames have different sizes?
differentSizes.a: You have three options for handling dimension mismatches. "Transparent" centers each frame on a transparent canvas sized to match the largest frame, which works well for sprites with varying bounding boxes. "Fill" does the same centering but fills the extra space with a background color of your choice (configurable via a color picker). "Error" rejects the conversion and returns a 400 error, prompting you to standardize frame dimensions before trying again — useful if you want to catch unintentional size differences.

gridVsCell.q: What's the difference between Grid and Cell slicing mode?
gridVsCell.a: Grid mode asks for the number of columns and rows in your spritesheet, then calculates the pixel size of each cell by dividing the image dimensions by the column and row counts. Cell mode works the other way — you provide the exact pixel width and height of each frame, and the tool calculates how many columns and rows fit. Use Grid mode when you know your sheet is organized as, say, 8 columns by 4 rows. Use Cell mode when you know each frame is exactly 64×64 pixels but aren't sure about the grid layout.

transparency.a: Yes, transparent regions in your PNG frames are preserved as GIF transparency in the output animation. However, note that GIF only supports binary transparency (fully transparent or fully opaque), unlike PNG which supports partial alpha transparency. Semi-transparent pixels in your PNG frames will be converted to either fully transparent or fully opaque in the GIF output.

free.a: Yes, this tool is completely free to use with no registration or account creation required. You can convert PNG frames or spritesheets to GIF as many times as you need. The tool is supported by occasional non-intrusive prompts and the companion API service on RapidAPI.

api.a: Yes. This tool is powered by the Easy GIF2Sprite API available on RapidAPI. The API offers two endpoints: /from-frames for assembling multiple PNG files into an animated GIF, and /from-spritesheet for slicing a spritesheet PNG into frames and producing an animated GIF. Both endpoints support the same parameters available in the web tool, making it straightforward to automate conversions in your build pipeline or application.
